We recommend that the Municipal Mayor:

Require the Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare the appropriated journal entries and other accounting records to effect the necessary adjustments on the erroneously classified ₱22,675,651.82 embankment projects and procured filling materials, (ii) compute the corresponding depreciation in order to rectify the errors and present the asset accounts to their proper carrying amount, (iii) consider the application of the Revaluation Model as accounting policy on the Land account of the PPE, with the assistance of the Municipal Assessor for the appraisal, to come up with a fair presentation of the account balance.

observations.recommendation:
1.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the established Inventory Committee, in collaboration with the
Municipal Engineer to conduct a comprehensive physical inspection
and count of all local road network projects and other properties in the
Municipality;
b. instruct the General Service Officer (GSO), to prepare the Report on
the Physical Count of Local Road Network, maintain and update Local
Road Inventory and Road Map annually and submit such reports to the
Municipal Accountant, copy furnished the Audit Team, for
evaluation; and
c. require the Municipal Accountant to immediately effect adjusting
journal entries to correct the accounting errors in accordance with the
provisions of Paragraph 82 of IPSAS 17.
1.13 In the Exit Conference, the Municipal Accountant affirmed the audit team’s
observation and recommendation on the erroneously derecognized PPE, and
agreed to promptly comply with the adjusting entries thereof. Meanwhile, the
Municipal Engineer and Inventory Committee Chairwoman established that the
deficiency was attributed to the unavailable records from prior years, but both
guaranteed coordination with the provincial government office for assistance on
this matter.

2.15 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a.) Municipal Accountant:
1. conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the individual
accounts under the Due to GSIS and Pag-IBIG accounts, exhaust
possible remedy to reconcile the negative and/or unsubstantiated
balances and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted; and
2. administer monthly reconciliations of employee premium
contributions and loan amortizations by comparing the GSIS and
Pag-IBIG billing against the actual payroll deductions and
remitted amounts. This ensures billing accuracy, facilitates timely
remittances, and guarantees correct salary deductions.
b.) Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO):
1. promptly coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to update personnel’s employment status
and/or salary adjustments. This will ensure that GSIS and Pag-
IBIG records reflect accurate and up-to-date information; and
2. mandate the unregistered LGU employees with GSIS and Pag-
IBIG to apply for membership to effect remittance of CY 2024 and
prior year’s contributions and ensure that employees receive their
entitled benefits and avoid unnecessary financial burden.
2.16 The HRMO and personnel overseeing GSIS contributions explained that
approval on the employment status and/or salary adjustments update in the
system takes a while, hence, the remittances are only based on the billings
provided regardless of the amount withheld and will then remit the difference
when the adjustments take effect in the system.
2.17 Moreover, the Municipal Accountant certified during the Exit Conference to
reconcile and/or substantiate the prior years’ balances, in collaboration with the
newly hired personnel in-charged for the remittances, to establish transparency
and achieve fair presentation of the Due to accounts in the financial statements.

3.14 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource
Management Officer (HRMO), mandate the concerned LGU personnel to
apply for membership with PhilHealth to effect remittance of CY 2024 and
prior year’s contributions and ensure that employees receive benefits due
them, without prejudice to the imposition of penalties as provided for in
Section 38 of R.A. No. 11223.
3.15 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unsettled
prior years' Due to PhilHealth balance of ₱140,348.43 and the
unsettled CY 2024 balance amounting to ₱53,093.67. Any confirmed
unsettled balances must be remitted to PhilHealth immediately, based
on the result of the recommendation above; and
b. deduct in the payroll the contribution for PhilHealth premiums of the
aforementioned LGU Officials and employees, and remit the same to
PhilHealth, together with the employer share.
3.16 The Municipal Accountant positively acknowledged the audit recommendations
and committed to take corrective/necessary actions on the omissions and/or
deficiencies noted affecting the fair presentation of the Due to PhilHealth
account.
3.17 On the other hand, the HRMO ensured to call the attention of the personnel
concerned to administer registration with PhilHealth, together with the Pag-
IBIG membership.

4.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource
Management Officer (HRMO), make amendments in the proforma
contract of Job Orders or COS personnel whose annual gross income
exceeds ₱250,000.00 that, unless the optional 8% Income Tax Rate per
RMC No. 51-2018 is availed, taxable income shall be subjected to the
Graduated Income Tax Rate prescribed in Section 24 of 1997 NIRC, as
amended by Republic Act No. 10963.
4.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with
the HRMO:
a. recognize the ₱41,340.90 over-remittance and facilitate the refund to the
entitled personnel;
b. immediately remit to the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) the
insufficient withholding tax of ₱2,500.00; and
c. effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the
correct balance of the "Due to BIR" account.
4.11 Both the Municipal Accountant and HRMO were receptive with the
recommendations and agreed to take into consideration the observations noted
by the Audit Team for future reference.

5.11 Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipality continuously adhere to
the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular
No. 2013-01, as amended by JMC No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016 to
optimize the purpose for which the fund was created, as conceptualized in
the Annual Gender and Development Plan and Budget (GPB)

5.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal
Person to:
provide a satisfactory explanation for the disbursement of funds
exceeding the allocated budget and/or without proper appropriation in
the approved Annual GPB; and 
ensure that appropriations are available before any disbursement and
that funds are disbursed strictly within the approved budget limits.

5.13 The GAD Focal Person justified that since identification of priority programs
and activities under the fund are usually done within the first quarter of the prior
year, inability to update the predetermined PAPs including the respective
proposed individual budget in the system was the reason for the overdraft.
Henceforth, coordination and technical assistance will be requested from the
system operators.

6.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the MPDO
Designate and Municipal Engineer, render a written justification why:
a.) there are inconsistencies between the approved Annual Budget and
Annual Investment Plan on identified priority development projects
for CY 2024; and
b.) the issuance of Agency’s Notice to Proceed was simultaneous with the
Statement of Work Accomplished provided by the contractor for the
first progress billing;
6.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Engineer, facilitate immediate payment of the ₱421,897.08 deficit in
contract amount to LC Ting Construction and Development Corporation,
to avoid possible future legal claims/disputes.
6.11 At the exit conference, the Municipal Engineer restated the oversight on
inadequate payment but guaranteed that the Municipality is in the process of
paying LC Ting Construction and Development Corporation the ₱421,897.08
deficit. Likewise, he explained that he was unaware of the synchronized LGU’s
issuance of NTP and Contractor’s SWA for the Grouted Riprap project at the
Civic Center and that he was only designated as MEO in the third quarter of
2024. Lastly, he and the Municipal Accountant assured the Audit Team that
proper validation and monitoring will be implemented prior to the release of
payment.
6.12 The MPDO Designate, on the other hand, justified that failure to communicate
the revisions with the Municipal Budget Officer was cause of the disagreement
between the Annual Budget and Annual Investment Plan. He also warranted that
the same circumstance will not occur again, if so, corresponding realignment
and/or revision shall be made.

7.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the
members of the Sanggunian and the Municipal Assessor, take the following
actions:

a. provide funds/budget for the processing of documents/requirements for
land titling and designate an LGU Personnel to facilitate processing of
land title;

b. exhaust possible remedy to obtain/secure a Certificate of Land Title or any
other proof of ownership for all lot purchased by and/or donated to the
Municipality; and

c. submit the necessary lacking documentary requirements to ascertain
propriety and regularity of the lot acquisition.

7.11 In the reply of the Municipal Assessor, dated March 4, 2025, land titling for lot
116 is on-going at CENRO, Ormoc. While, she clarified that only a total land
area of 762 sqms. for lots 116 and 124 was acquired by the Municipality due to
the sellers ₱2-Million demand for the three lots, which former could not defray.
Together with her reply were Certified Photocopies of the Deed of Absolute
Sale and other relevant documents needed for free DENR titling application.

8.22 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a.) the IRB Enterprises to promptly accomplish the delivery of the DRRM
Equipment; and

b.) the BAC and MDRRM Officer to closely monitor the supplier’s
fulfillment of the above recommendation.

In case of noncompliance, the LGU should cancel the partial payment,
terminate the contract, forfeit the performance security and further
recommend for blacklisting and perpetual disqualification from
participating in any public bidding as provided under Section 69 and
Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184; and

c.) the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidation Damages, equal to one-
tenth (1/10) of one percent (1%) of the delayed DRR Rescue and OpCen
Equipment costing ₱515,564.00.

d.) the GSO and BAC to ensure complete and appropriate documentation
pursuant to R.A. 9184 with completely filled-out PR, PO and IAR.

8.23 Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and
wastage of government funds and resources, we recommended that the
following measures for future projects of similar nature be undertaken:

a.) Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for
infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the
approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in
the contract agreement; and

b.) Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If
so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions
particularly termination of contracts and blacklisting of contractors
for failure to comply with their obligations in accordance with the
provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the Revised IRR
of R.A. 9184.

9.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource
and Management Officer and Municipal Treasurer:
a.) adopt electronic payroll system through the Automated Teller
Machine (ATM) of Government Banks for the payment of wages of
Job Orders and Contract of Service personnel; and
b.) ensure that wages of Job Orders and Contract of Service personnel
conform with the mandated daily minimum wage rate pursuant to
Wage Order No. RB VIII-24 of DOLE National Wages and
Productivity Commission Regional Tripartite Wages and
Productivity Board.

9.14 The Municipal Treasurer, who also is the designated disbursing officer,
enthusiastically uphold to the above electronic payroll system recommendation
and have even coordinated with the Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP)
for the requirements and due process. Meanwhile, the HRMO reiterated that
budget constraints is the very reason for the LGU’s noncompliance with the
RTWPB’s prescribed daily minimum wage and she likewise justified that
despite the number of JOs and COS personnel, insufficient manpower still exist.

10.6 We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe the provisions of
Department of Budget and Management Budget Circular No. 2024-1 dated
April 2, 2024. Otherwise, the personnel concerned refund the amount
received, without prejudice to the joint and solidary liability of those who
authorized and certified the regularity and propriety of the said payment.

11.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all insurable LGU
properties are covered under the General Insurance Fund (GIF) of the
Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) to ensure that any loss or
damage to the same will be indemnified by the insurer pursuant to Republic
Act No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular 2018-002.

11.8 During the Exit Conference, the Municipality was amenable and agreed to
consider the recommendation of the Audit team, dependent on the availability
of funds.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to:

Coordinate with each other and reconcile their records at least every end of each quarter and any items for adjustments must be corrected and resolved immediately to present a reliable Cash in Bank and Cash Local Treasury balances;

Ensure that regular and timely reconciliation between the records of the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant is strictly observed so that errors or any other reconciling items will be immediately corrected/updated; and

As a future reference, examine meticulously the policy on the reconciliation of the records in the cashbooks of the Municipal Treasurer with the subsidiary ledgers of the Municipal Accountant.

We also recommend that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the cashbooks of all the accounts of the municipality are regularly updated.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

Ensure adherence to the provisions of International Public Sector Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 17 and relevant/applicable COA Circulars and other references.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor requires:

The Municipal Accountant to ensure that (i) all cash advance vouchers and complete required supporting documents are submitted within seven (7) days after release of check, in a sealed envelope, to the Audit Team for post audit; and (ii) effect adjusting entries for the deficiencies noted and henceforth, ensure that all transactions are properly recorded in their appropriate accounts; and

The Municipal Treasurer, as Special Disbursing Officer, to prepare and submit the Liquidation Report of the Cash Advances supported with documentary requirements to the ICFAU within the prescribed period, in a sealed envelope with a visible label “CONFIDENTIAL  - For ICFAU Only”.

We also recommend that management strictly adhere to the provisions of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 to ensure proper budgeting, utilization and reporting of the confidential funds.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

MDRRMO, in collaboration with the MDRRM Council, to (i) identify and prioritize projects which are realistic, urgent and doable based on their capacity for inclusion in the current year LDRRMF Investment Plan, recognizing prior years’ unexpended DRRM Fund, and update/revise as necessary to effect the changes in the PPAs to be implemented; and (ii) urge the MDRRMO to implement all programs and projects in the LDRRMF Investment Plan for each year to improve and enhance its disaster preparedness and response capabilities in times of calamity and protect the safety and wellbeing of the residents; and (iii) religiously submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF within the prescribed period per Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee, to closely monitor the implementation of planned development projects of the 20% Development Fund and adopt methods to ensure the timely conduct of the procurement process to optimally utilize the fund and eventually help the municipality in timely attaining its desired goals and objectives of the said fund.

As a future reference, we recommend that the municipality comply with the governing rules and regulations which prescribe the submission of the AAPSI within sixty (60) days upon receipt of the AAR CY 2023 and its ensuing annual reports.

We reiterated the recommendation that the:

The Municipal Mayor give priority in establishing a Supply Storage Area to ensure the safekeeping of goods delivered to the Municipality, which will greatly aid in the proper management of the Municipality's inventories.

The Municipal General Services Office (MGSO), as the head of the Inventory Committee, take the following actions: 1) initiate a physical count of all inventory items within 30 days upon receipt of the Annual Audit Report (AAR) for CY 2022, and prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation purposes. Afterward, conduct a physical count of all inventory items every end of the semester; 2) prepare the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) when distributing/issuing stocks to the end-users and consolidate all RIS in the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued. Submit the said report to the Municipal Accountant as the basis for recognizing expenses in the books.

The Municipal Accountant should cease the practice of immediately recording inventories received by the municipality into expense accounts. Instead, recognize the corresponding expense account based on the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.

We also recommended that the MGSO create and maintain updated Stock Cards for each category of supplies with their respective stock numbers. These Stock Cards should be reconciled with the RPCI at the end of each year.

Moreover, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant should maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for every stock number and reconcile them with the Stock Cards of the GSO and the RPCI of the Inventory Committee.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor assign a capable regular/permanent staff to: 1) oversee the organization and monitoring of drug, medicine, medical, and laboratory supplies inventory and records in the RHU; and 2) enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of GSO in performing its obligations and functions in managing and maintaining the municipality's resources.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instruct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of PPE owned by the Municipality, identifying the proper classification, individual identification, status of serviceability, accountability and location thereof; (ii) prepare and submit a complete RPCPPE to the Municipal Accountant not later than January 31 of each year, copy furnish the Audit Team; and (iii) prepare and submit IIRUP together with its supporting documents for disposal of unserviceable properties;

require the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite substantiation of recorded unidentified PPE items based on the result of inventory count as recommended above; (ii) make the necessary adjustments in the books to correct erroneously classified ₱2,498,863.50 Drainage Canal at Municipal Compound; (iii) drop from the PPE account the identified items below the ₱15,000.00 threshold totalling ₱3,840,477.22; (iv) initiate the proper turn-over of the completed projects to the respective Barangay-Recipients supported with the properly accomplished turn-over documents, and effect adjustments in the books; and (v) prepare/maintain and update PPE Ledger Cards for each category of assets and Real Property Ledger Cards for land; and

direct the General Services Officer to take charge in setting up and updating of Property Cards for proper monitoring, control and accountability of purchased/ acquired and issued PPEs.

We also recommended that the Municipality avail the one-time cleansing of the PPE Account and strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant generate a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (DRRMF), and ensure that any Trust Liability related to the DRRM account is duly corrected. Further, we advise strict compliance with COA Circular 2012-002 issued on September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain comprehensive subsidiary records for all fund received for each project.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:

direct the resident/project engineers to (i) conduct thorough inspection on all infrastructure projects for validation of the actual accomplishment submitted for progress billing to make sure that all materials and prescribed procedures conform with the Program of Work, Detailed Estimates and Plan or Variation Orders, if any; and (ii) prepare Inspection Report accurately showing the actual work accomplished as compared to the contractor’s SWA and make necessary adjustments/deductions from the progress billing equivalent to the cost of noted inaccuracy.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

Bids and Awards Committee to strictly adhere with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184, existing law, rules and regulations including COA Circulars and latest GPPB Resolutions to ensure that all awarded contracts be supported with sufficient and/or appropriate documents; and 

Municipal Engineer to require inclusion of Occupational Safety and Health as separate pay item in every infrastructure project implemented in compliance with DPWH D.O. No. 56 s. 2005 dated April 20, 2005 and monitor the proper implementation of the said program to ensure its compliance with the requirements of the DOLE and that workers are adequately protected against occupational risks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

ensure that all vehicles owned by the Municipality are promptly registered as government vehicles with the LTO under the name of the Municipality, include in the annual budget appropriation for the renewal of registration payment of all vehicles, and registration be made within the period prescribed by the LTO to avoid payment of penalties and other sanctions; and

instruct the Property Custodian or Driver of the Motor Vehicle to stop the operation/use of the vehicles with expired LTO registration.

We also recommended that the duly appointed GSO:

effect immediate renewal of the three (3) vehicles operating with invalid LTO registration;

investigate, locate records and submit copies of registration documents for the 13 motor vehicles;

keep the records of registration for all LGU vehicles, monitor their expiry dates and schedule the annual renewal of LTO registration in coordination with the Property Custodian or Driver of the vehicle; and

evaluate any unserviceable vehicles for possible disposal, and in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, follow the disposal procedures unserviceable vehicles, pursuant to pertinent provisions in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of PPE in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 and instruct the Chairperson of the Inventory Committee to:

prepare and submit to the Municipal Mayor for his approval, a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) indicating therein the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, cut-off date and schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory;

conduct the physical inventory within the period identified in the PIP and tag all counted properties of the municipality;

categorize the accounted PPE using the PPSAS account classification in the RPCPPE and prepare the report within 60 days from the completion of the physical inventory and furnish a copy of the report to the COA Team Leader, Municipal Accountant and Head of the Property Unit of the Municipality; and

prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and complete the disposal of the unserviceable properties.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer conduct a reconciliation of the PPEs included in the RPCPPE with the recorded properties in the books of accounts of the Municipality and prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the Municipal Mayor for his appropriate action, in cases of non-existing/missing properties, if any.
We recommended finally that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer, perform the procedures to account for non-existing/missing properties as a result of the reconciliation conducted to facilitate the disposition of these items in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 to reflect a reliable balance of the PPE account in the Financial Statement.

We recommended that the MGSO, as head of the Inventory Committee, initiate the conduct of physical count of all inventory items within 30 days from receipt of the Annual Audit Report (AAR) for CY 2022 and to prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation purposes.  Thereafter, conduct the physical count of all inventory items of the Municipality every end of the semester.

We also recommended that the MGSO prepare the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon distribution/issuance of the stocks to the end users and summarize all RIS in the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit to the Municipal Accountant as basis for the recognition of the expenses in the books.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of recording immediately to expense accounts the inventories received by the municipality. Recognize the corresponding expense account based on the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, prioritize the establishment of a Supply Office to ensure the safekeeping of goods delivered to the Municipality and which will greatly help in the proper management of the inventories of the municipality.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create the four statutory positions for the LDRRM Office to perform the functions as mandated by the regulations, subject to PS limitation of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: 2 Positions have already been created, 1 of which is already filled-up, the other one is still vacant

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) pay the prescribed daily minimum wage as prescribed by the Regional Tripartite Wages and Productivity Boards for Region VIII.

We recommended that the HRMO update and reconcile with the GSIS the records of all employees of the Municipality of Matag-ob on their premium contributions and status of loans to confirm any under/over-remittance of the LGU to the said agency.  Also, provide the GSIS immediately with appropriate documents on changes of employees’ salaries so that GSIS billings will be accurate.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO resolve the prior years’ balances on GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations by December 31, 2020 so that all LGU Matag-ob employees can fully avail of the GSIS benefits, avoid additional loan interests and surcharges and confirm that the amount paid for the employees’ loans were not taken from LGU funds.  Also, reconcile monthly their records to ensure accurate remittances.

trace back prior years’ transactions with Pag-IBIG Fund to resolve the negative balances of loan amortization payments of the employees to confirm that the amount paid for their loans were not taken from LGU funds; and 

reconcile monthly the SL against the source documents on transactions with Pag-IBIG Fund to avoid accumulation of variances.

observations.recommendation:
1.10. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer
and the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Coordinate with each other and reconcile their records at least every end
of each quarter and any items for adjustment must be corrected and
resolved immediately to present a reliable Cash in Bank and Cash Local
Treasury balances;
b. Ensure that regular and timely reconciliation between the records of the
Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant is strictly observed
so that errors or any other reconciling items will be immediately
corrected/updated; and
c. As a future reference, examine meticulously the policy on the
reconciliation of the records in the cashbooks of the Municipal Treasurer
with the subsidiary ledgers of the Municipal Accountant.

1.1 We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the cashbooks
of all the accounts of the municipality are regularly updated.

1.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:

a. recompute the depreciation based on the changes in accounting
estimates due to the alteration of the residual value as prescribed by
Philippine Application Guidelines (PAG) 6 of IPSAS 17 and apply the same
in prospective application on the period following the audit year;
b. prepare the appropriate journal entries and other accounting records
to correct the depreciation and effect the necessary adjustments in order to
rectify the errors and present the asset accounts to their proper carrying
amount; and
c. ensure adherence to the provisions of International Public Sector
Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 17 and relevant/applicable COA Circulars
and other references.

3.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. require the Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare the appropriate
journal entries and other accounting records to effect the necessary
adjustments on the erroneously classified ₱22,675,651.82
embankment projects and procured filling materials, (ii) compute the
corresponding depreciation in order to rectify the errors and present
the asset accounts to their proper carrying amount, (iii) consider the
application of the Revaluation Model as accounting policy on the
Land account of the PPE, with the assistance of the Municipal
Assessor for the appraisal, to come up with a fair presentation of the
account balance; and
b. direct the GSO and Inventory Committee to identify land/real
properties owned by the Municipality and conduct a comprehensive
physical inventory to ascertain the existence, rights, and obligations
of the recorded land account items.

4.9 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant to identify the creditors comprising the accounts payable as of
CY 2023 to establish the propriety and the validity of the accounts payable,
otherwise, the same should be reverted or be written-off if there are no
legitimate claimants.

5.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor requires:

a. the Municipal Accountant to ensure that (i) all cash advance vouchers
and complete required supporting documents are submitted within
seven (7) days after release of check, in a sealed envelope, to the Audit
Team for post audit; and (ii) effect adjusting entries for the
deficiencies noted and henceforth, ensure that all transactions are
properly recorded in their appropriate accounts; and
b. the Municipal Treasurer, as Special Disbursing Officer, to prepare
and submit the Liquidation Report of the Cash Advances supported
with documentary requirements to the ICFAU within the prescribed
period, in a sealed envelope with a visible label “CONFIDENTIAL –
For ICFAU Only”.

5.12 We also recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions of
the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 to ensure
proper budgeting, utilization and reporting of the confidential funds.

6.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a.) MDRRMO, in collaboration with the MDRRM Council, to (i) identify
and prioritize projects which are realistic, urgent, and doable based
on their capacity for inclusion in the current year LDRRMF
Investment Plan, recognizing prior years’ unexpended DRRM Fund,
and update/revise as necessary to effect the changes in the PPAs to be
implemented; and (ii) urge the MDRRMO to implement all programs
and projects in the LDRRMF Investment Plan for each year to
improve and enhance its disaster preparedness and response
capabilities in times of calamity and protect the safety and wellbeing
of the residents; and (iii) religiously submit the Report on Sources and
Utilization of DRRMF within the prescribed period per Section 5.1.5
of COA Circular No. 2012-002;
b.) Municipal Accountant to (i) immediately effect the necessary
adjustments in the Special Trust Fund to rectify the errors and
present the accounts to their proper/ accurate balances; and (ii)
transfer only unexpended balances of the Quick Response Fund and
LDRRMF for MOOE to the Special Trust Fund account; and
c.) MDRRMO, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to
investigate the noted inconsistency and make sure that their
respective registry/reports are reconciled.

6.13 We also recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions of
the COA Circular 2012-002 issued on September 12, 2012 and relevant/
applicable references on the utilization and implementation of the 5%
LDRRM Fund to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency,
effectiveness and efficiency.

7.11 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with the Bids and
Awards Committee, to closely monitor the implementation of planned
development projects of the 20% Development Fund and adopt methods to
ensure the timely conduct of the procurement process to optimally utilize the
fund and eventually help the Municipality in timely attaining its desired
goals and objectives of the said Fund.

8.12 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned
accountable officers:

a. To submit the noted lacking supporting documents and strictly
observe the required documentation prescribed under the foregoing
rules and regulations; and
b. As a future reference, examine meticulously the rules and regulations
on the grant, utilization, liquidation, and reporting of financial
assistance received from the National Government Agencies. In
addition, instruct the submission of the required monthly Reports on
Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project/Activity (P/P/A) of the
Financial Assistance in compliance with the governing rules and
regulations.

9.7. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned
personnel to prepare and submit the AAPSI for the AAR CY 2022.

9.8. As a future reference, we recommend that the municipality comply with the
governing rules and regulation which prescribe the submission of the AAPSI
within sixty (60) days upon receipt of the AAR CY 2023 and its ensuing
annual reports.

The Municipal Treasurer shall strictly require the daily and intact remittance of collections from the revenue collectors.
Stop the practice of issuing additional accountable forms while collections out of the previously issued forms were not yet remitted.
Impose disciplinary actions/sanctions to those collectors who repeatedly commit the same violation.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to make a demand for the liquidation of the funds transferred to barangays who have completed the implementation of projects; and
Require the personnel concerned of the Barangays to submit an explanation detailing the reason/s on the delay of the implementation of the projects and the submission of liquidation report.

We recommended that the Management thru the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office monitor the utilization of LDRRMF focusing on the program/projects/activities (PPAs) requiring the highest degree of priority in terms of urgency or impact on the four thematic areas of DRRM. Likewise, ensure efficient and effective implementation of the programmed PPAs during the year to the utilization of the fund, so that desired goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be optimally achieved in compliance with NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended that the Management thru the Municipal Planning & Development Coordinator enhance the monitoring of the timely implementation of the projects, proper planning, and coordination with the concerned officials for efficient and economical utilization of government funds and to attain the desired objective of the projects.

We recommended that the Management exert the necessary efforts in order to finalize its Ten-year Solid Waste Management Plan and be subjected to the approval of the National Solid Waste Management Commission.
We recommended that the Management submit the required documents for the purchase of land and fast track the process of transferring the title in the name of the local government unit.
We recommended that the Management impose legal remedies on the collection of delinquent taxes to increase/improve revenue collections.

We recommended that the Management through its GFPS administer the gender analysis using the HGDG on its GAD PPAs during the preparation of the GPB as well as on the GAD AR to determine the actual expenditures that can be attributed to GAD budget pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JM No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the law, particularly on the early remittance of premium contributions to the HDMF so that the municipality could avoid the payment of for late remittance of premiums.
We recommended the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary reconciliation on the reciprocal accounts and take up necessary adjusting entries to correct the reported balances.
